1: šîrat dôdî = "song of my beloved/love-song." Šîr (H7892) = song; dôd (H1730) = beloved, loved one (the word of the Song of Solomon). The prophecy opens as a love poem, luring the audience before the legal indictment unfolds. karmô = "his vineyard" — kerem (H3754) always implies cultivated care and investment. bĕqeren ben-šāmen = "on a horn of a son of oil/fertility" — the most fertile hillside, a sunny south-facing slope. Every advantage was given.
2:
wayyĕʿazkĕqēhû — Piel of ʿāzaq (H5823) = to dig/cultivate thoroughly, a rare verb found only here. wayyĕsaqqĕlēhû — Piel of sāqal = to clear of stones — a major labor investment. šôrēq (H8321) = the finest variety of red grape vine. migdāl = watchtower; yāqeḇ = wine vat hewn in rock. Every element — clearing, planting, tower, vat — demonstrates total preparatory commitment.

wayyĕqaw laʿăśôt ʿănāḇîm wayyaʿaś bĕʾušîm = "he waited (qāwâ = hope-laden waiting) for grapes, but it produced wild grapes (bĕʾušîm)." Bĕʾušîm (H891) = worthless, stinking fruit — the antithesis of šôrēq. The disproportion between investment and return is the legal complaint.
3: šipṭû-nāʾ bênî ûḇên karmî = "judge (šāpaṭ, imperative), please, between me and my vineyard." The audience is summoned as jury in the rîb (lawsuit). The rhetorical brilliance: they render judgment before realizing they are judging themselves.
4: mah laʿăśôt ʿôd lĕkarmî wĕlōʾ ʿāśîtî bô = "What more was there to do for my vineyard that I have not done in it?" The question presupposes the answer: nothing. Every possible investment was made. The failure is entirely in the vineyard. This is the theological logic of prevenient grace preceding covenant failure.
5: wĕʿattâ ʾôdîʿâ-nāʾ ʾetkem ʾēt ʾăšer-ʾănî ʿōśeh lĕkarmî = "And now let me tell you what I am about to do to my vineyard." The verdict: since the vineyard failed despite total care, the protection (mĕśûkâ = hedge; gādēr = wall) will be removed. Without the hedge, it becomes trampling-ground (mirmās).
6:
waʾăśîtēhû bātâ = "I will make it a waste." lōʾ yizzāmēr wĕlōʾ yēʿādēr = "it will not be pruned and not be hoed" — abandonment. wĕʿālu šāmîr wāšît = "thorns and briers shall come up" — šāmîr wāšît is Isaiah's repeated pair for curse-reversed-fruitfulness (Isaiah 7:23-25; Isaiah 9:17; Isaiah 10:17; Isaiah 27:4; Isaiah 32:13).

wĕʿal heʿābîm ʾăṣawweh = "I will command the clouds not to rain on it" — YHWH commands the weather, unmasking his control of the cosmos. Israel's land productivity depends on covenant relationship with the one who controls rain (cf. Deuteronomy 11:14; Isaiah 28:12,24).
7: kî kerem YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt bêt yiśrāʾēl — the allegory decoded: vineyard = Israel, choice vine = Judah. wayyĕqaw lĕmišpāṭ wĕhinnēh miśpāḥ — the devastating Hebrew wordplay: mišpāṭ (justice) → miśpāḥ (bloodshed); ṣĕdāqâ (righteousness) → ṣĕʿāqâ (cry of the oppressed). The near-rhyme makes the contrast both memorable and cutting: YHWH expected justice, found bloodshed; expected righteousness, heard the shriek of the oppressed.
8: hôy (H1945) = the woe-oracle particle, originally a funeral cry, used by prophets to pronounce coming judgment as though it were already death. First woe targets land accumulation: maggîšê bayit bĕbayit = "those who join house to house." Land consolidation violated Mosaic land law (Lev 25; Numbers 27:1-11), under which each family held inalienable tribal allotment. ʿad ʾepem māqôm = "until there is no more place" — economic monopoly described as spatial displacement of the poor.
9: The divine oath formula: bĕʾoznāy YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt = "in my ears, LORD of armies" — Isaiah hears YHWH swear. bāttîm rabbîm lĕšammâ yihyû = "many houses shall become a desolation (šammâ)" — the accumulated houses become the judgment. The collector of houses will find them all empty; the "great and fine" houses (gĕdōlîm wĕṭōḇîm) become great vacancies.
10: kî ʿăśeret ṣimĕdê kerem yaʿăśû bat ʾeḥāt = "ten yokes of vineyard will produce one bath." A ṣemed = the area one yoke of oxen plows in a day. A single bath (ca. 6 gallons) from ten plots is catastrophic failure — a single plot should produce 300+ liters. wĕzeraʿ ḥōmer yaʿăśeh ʾêpâ = "a homer of seed will produce an ephah" — one-tenth yield. They hoarded land for agricultural dominance; the land itself will resist them.
11: Second woe: early-rising drinkers. maškîmê babbōqer šēkār yirdōpû = "those who rise early to pursue (rādap = chase after) strong drink (šēkār)." The urgency of maškîmê (to rise early, H7925) applied to alcohol consumption rather than prayer or work satirizes misplaced devotion. mĕʾaḥărê bannešep = "who tarry late in the evening" — bookending the day with drunkenness.
12: wĕhāyâ kinnôr wāneḇel tōp wĕḥālîl wāyyin mištêhem = "the harp, lute, tambourine, flute, and wine are at their feasts." The full musical catalog — kinnôr (lyre), neḇel (harp), tōp (tambourine), ḥālîl (flute) — is not condemned as such but as a substitute for attending to YHWH's work in history. wĕʾēt pōʿal YHWH lōʾ yaḇbiṭû = "but they do not regard the work (pōʿal) of YHWH" — to miss God's work in history is spiritual blindness at the deepest level.
13: lākēn gālâ ʿammî mibbelî-dāʿat = "Therefore my people will go into exile for lack of knowledge (bĕlî-dāʿat)." Gālâ (H1540) = to go into exile — the technical term for Assyrian/Babylonian deportations. The cause: not factual ignorance but failure of relational daʿat (knowing YHWH, cf. Isaiah 1:3; Hosea 4:6). wĕkaḇĕdô mĕtê rāʿāb = "their honored ones will die of famine" — social reversal: wealthy feasters will starve.
14: lākēn hirḥîḇâ šĕʾôl napšāh = "therefore Sheol has enlarged its throat (napšāh, lit. its soul/throat)." Sheol is personified as an insatiable monster opening its gullet (cf. Habakkuk 2:5). The death-as-consuming-beast motif appears in Canaanite mythology (Mot); Isaiah deploys it to describe the scale of coming judgment where even the honored (v13) are swallowed.
15: wayyišaḥ ʾādām wayyišpal-ʾîš = "mankind will be humbled (šāḥâ) and people brought low (šāpal)" — both verbs describe descending from height, reversing the exaltation sought by the land-grabbers and revelers. wĕʿênê gĕḇōhîm tišpalnâ = "and the eyes of the haughty will be brought low" — the proud gaze that looked down at the poor is itself brought down. This reversal-of-pride theology is central to Isaiah's theodicy (cf. Isaiah 2:12-17).
16: wayyigbaĥ YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt bammišpāṭ = "but YHWH of hosts is exalted (gāḇah) in justice (mišpāṭ)." Ironic reversal: man descends, YHWH ascends — in the very act of judgment. wĕhāʾēl haqqādôš niqdaš biṣĕdāqâ = "the Holy God shows himself holy (niqdaš, niphal reflexive of qādaš) in righteousness." Holiness is not merely a status; it is demonstrated in righteous action against injustice.
17: wĕrāʿû ḵĕḇāśîm kĕdoḇrām = "and lambs shall graze as in their pasture." After human judgment, nature reclaims what was over-cultivated by the greedy. wĕḥurāḇôt mēʾîm gārîm yōʾkēlû = "and the ruins of the fat ones (mēʾîm) will be eaten by migrants (gārîm)" — the accumulated wealth of the wealthy becomes food/pasture for those they displaced. Poetic justice in agricultural terms.
18: Third woe: those who drag sin behind them like a cart. hôy môšĕkê heʿāwōn bĕḥaḇlê haššāwĕʾ = "woe to those who drag iniquity (ʿāwōn) with cords (ḥeḇel) of falsehood (šāwĕʾ)" — sin is a burden requiring effort to pull. ʿĀwōn = guilt/iniquity/punishment; ḥaḇlê šāwĕʾ = ropes of nothingness. wĕkaʿăḇôt hāʿăgālâ ḥaṭṭāʾâ = "and sin as with cart (ʿăgālâ) ropes (ʿăḇôt)" — cart ropes are thicker, heavier; sin has become structural, load-bearing.
19: hāʾōmĕrîm yĕmaher yāḥîšâ maʿăśēhû = "who say: let him hurry, let him hasten his work" — they mock YHWH's slowness to judge, daring him to act. wĕtiqraḇ wĕtāḇôʾ ʿăṣat qĕdôš yiśrāʾēl = "and let the plan (ʿēṣâ) of the Holy One of Israel draw near." Qĕdôš yiśrāʾēl = "Holy One of Israel" — one of Isaiah's signature divine epithets (25+ occurrences in Isaiah vs. 6 in all other OT books), linking holiness to the divine plan that the mockers challenge.
20: Fourth woe — the most conceptually radical. hôy hāʾōmĕrîm lārāʿ ṭôḇ wĕlaṭṭôḇ rāʿ = "woe to those who call evil good and good evil." Not merely moral confusion but category inversion — deliberate redefinition of moral categories. śāmîm ḥōšek lĕʾôr wĕʾôr lĕḥōšek = "who put darkness for light and light for darkness" — cosmological inversion. śāmîm mar lĕmāṯôq ûmāṭôq lĕmar = "who put bitter for sweet and sweet for bitter." The reversal descends from moral (good/evil) to cosmic (light/dark) to sensory (bitter/sweet): total perversion of perceivable reality.
21: Fifth woe: intellectual pride. hôy ḥăkāmîm bĕʿênêhem ûneḡed pĕnêhem nĕḇōnîm = "woe to those who are wise in their own eyes (bĕʿênêhem) and shrewd in their own sight." The reflexive self-certification: wisdom that only looks at itself cannot correct itself. Compare Proverbs 26:12 ("there is more hope for a fool than for a man wise in his own eyes") and Romans 12:16 ("do not be wise in your own sight").
22: Sixth woe: judicial corruption by those who pride themselves on drinking capacity. hôy gibbôrê lišĕtôt yāyin wĕʾanšê-ḥayil limSōk šēkār = "woe to those who are heroes (gibbôrê) at drinking wine and champions (ʾanšê-ḥāyil) at mixing strong drink." The irony: gibbôr = mighty man/military hero — the highest honor title now applied to drinking prowess. The warriors who should defend justice are drunk.
23: maṣdîqê rāšāʿ ʿēqeḇ šōḥad = "who acquit (ṣādaq, hiphil) the wicked for a bribe (šōḥad)" — hiphil of ṣādaq = to declare righteous, to acquit; here of the perversion where payment purchases legal vindication of the guilty. wĕṣidqat ṣaddîqîm yāsîrû mimmennû = "and take away the righteousness of the righteous" — the innocent condemned while the guilty are acquitted. This concrete charge explains all six woes: drunken leaders sell verdicts.
24: lākēn kiʾekol-qāš lĕšôn ʾēš = "therefore as the tongue of fire devours stubble" — judgment metaphors: fire on qāš (stubble) and ḥāšaš (chaff); rot in the root. Simultaneous destruction from above (fire on blossoms) and below (rot in root). kî māʾăsû ʾet-tôrat YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt = "because they have rejected the tôrâ of YHWH of hosts" — the summary charge. Nāʾaṣ = to spurn, treat with contempt. All six woes rest on this single root: refusal of YHWH's tôrâ.
25: wayyiḥar-ʾap YHWH bĕʿammô = "and the anger (ʾap, lit. "nose/nostril" = anger by synecdoche) of YHWH burned against his people." wayyēṭ yādô ʿālāw wayyakkēhû = "he stretched out his hand against them and struck them" — the same hand-extended formula as the Exodus plagues (Exodus 7:5; Isaiah 9:15), now turned against Israel. bĕkol-zōʾt lōʾ-šāḇ ʾappô = "for all this, his anger has not turned away" — the refrain repeated in Isaiah 9:12,17,21; Isaiah 10:4, marking that judgment has not yet exhausted itself. This verse prepares the extended Assyrian-invasion section (Isaiah 7-12).
26: wĕnāśāʾ-nēs laggôyim mērāḥôq = "and he will raise a signal/banner (nēs) for nations from far away." Nēs = the military signal banner raised to summon troops. ûšĕraq lô miqqĕṣēh hāʾāreṣ = "and he will whistle (šārak) for it from the ends of the earth" — the shepherd's call or beekeeper's call to swarm. YHWH calls Assyria with the ease of summoning animals. wĕhinnēh mĕhērâ qal yāḇôʾ = "and behold, it comes swiftly" — the response is instant.
27: Military description of the Assyrian army: ʾên-ʿāyēp wĕʾên-kôšēl bô = "none is weary and none stumbles" — a formidable, disciplined machine. lōʾ yānûm wĕlōʾ yîšān = "it does not slumber and does not sleep" — the ironically god-like quality of the invader (cf. Psalms 121:4 where only YHWH never sleeps). The enemy that never sleeps is YHWH's instrument, not a divine rival.
28: ʾăšer ḥiṣṣāyw šĕnûnîm = "whose arrows (ḥēṣ) are sharpened." kol-qăšĕtōtāw dĕrûkôt = "all his bows are bent/strung" — drawn and ready. pārāšāyw = cavalry; sus = horse. The description builds an impression of a fully armed, mechanically precise war machine — opposite of the drunken, sleepy leaders of the woe oracles.
29: šĕʾāgâ lô kaллābîʾ = "its roaring is like a lion (laḇîʾ, a lion in its prime)" — šāʾag = the triumph-roar of the successful hunter. wayyiśʾ kĕkĕpîrîm wayyinhōm = "it growls like young lions" — not one lion but the pride. wayyiśʾ tereṗ wĕyaʿăḇōr = "it seizes prey and carries it off, and none rescues." YHWH himself has become the lion that hunts through his Assyrian instrument.
30: wĕyinham ʿālāw bayyôm hahûʾ kĕhēmôt yām = "it will growl over them on that day like the roaring of the sea" — the sea as chaos-power. wĕnibbaṭ lāʾāreṣ wĕhinnēh-ḥōšek ṣar = "and if one looks to the earth, behold darkness and distress." The chapter ends at the lowest point: ḥōšek (H2822) and ʿărāpel (H6205) = thick/deep darkness. Yet the darkness gives way (cf. Isaiah 9:1-2): the chapter ends before the Immanuel oracle begins.
